Talk to Your Kids - The New Yorker

The New Yorker · by Margaret Talbot · January 5, 2015
Search
Annals of Education
January 5, 2015

One morning in September, Lissette Castrill\xc3\xb3n, a caseworker in Providence, Rhode Island, drove to an apartment on the western edge of town to visit Annie Rodriguez, a young mother, and her two-year-old daughter, Eilen. Castrill\xc3\xb3n and Rodriguez sat down on a worn rug and spoke about the importance of talking to very young children. They discussed ways to cajole a toddler into an extended conversation, and identified moments in the day when Rodriguez could be chatting more with Eilen, an ebullient little girl who was wearing polka-dot leggings.

\xe2\x80\x9cWhenever she\xe2\x80\x99s saying a few new words, it\xe2\x80\x99s important to tell her yes, and add to it,\xe2\x80\x9d Castrill\xc3\xb3n told Rodriguez. \xe2\x80\x9cSo if she sees a car you can say, \xe2\x80\x98Yes, that\xe2\x80\x99s a car. It\xe2\x80\x99s a big car. It\xe2\x80\x99s a blue car.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

Eilen suddenly said, \xe2\x80\x9cBoo ca!\xe2\x80\x9d

Castrill\xc3\xb3n looked at her and said, \xe2\x80\x9cRight! Blue car! Good job!\xe2\x80\x9d

Rodriguez noted that Eilen had recently become so enthralled by an animated show, \xe2\x80\x9cBubble Guppies,\xe2\x80\x9d that she had become \xe2\x80\x9cstuck on that word \xe2\x80\x98guppy.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d She went on, \xe2\x80\x9cEverything\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x98guppy, guppy, guppy.\xe2\x80\x99 So when she refers to something as \xe2\x80\x98guppy\xe2\x80\x99 I try to correct it\xe2\x80\x94like, \xe2\x80\x98No, that\xe2\x80\x99s not a guppy. That\xe2\x80\x99s a doll.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cGuppy?\xe2\x80\x9d Eilen said, hopefully.

Castrill\xc3\xb3n said, \xe2\x80\x9cWell, I think right now the important thing won\xe2\x80\x99t be so much telling her no but just adding words and repeating them, so she\xe2\x80\x99ll start repeating them on her own.\xe2\x80\x9d

Rodriguez is enrolled in a program called Providence Talks, the most ingenious of several new programs across the country that encourage low-income parents to talk more frequently with their kids. Once a month, Eilen wears a small recording device for the day, and the recording is then analyzed. An algorithm tallies all the words spoken by adults in her vicinity, all the vocalizations Eilen makes, and all the \xe2\x80\x9cconversational turns\xe2\x80\x9d\xe2\x80\x94exchanges in which Eilen says something and an adult replies, or vice versa. The caseworker who visits Rodriguez\xe2\x80\x99s home gives her a progress report, which shows in graph form how many words Eilen has been hearing, and how they peak and dip throughout the day.

Castrill\xc3\xb3n presented Rodriguez with the month\xe2\x80\x99s report. She leaned over her shoulder and said, \xe2\x80\x9cSee, this shows the percentage of adult words. There were over fifteen thousand words spoken in that day.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cWow!\xe2\x80\x9d Rodriguez said.

Castrill\xc3\xb3n noted that significantly more conversation took place when the TV was off, and that it had been off more that month than the previous one. \xe2\x80\x9cThere was pretty high electronic sound last time,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cThis time, there was very little.\xe2\x80\x9d Rodriguez nodded, studying the printout.

In the nineteen-eighties, two child psychologists at the University of Kansas, Betty Hart and Todd Risley, began comparing, in detail, how parents of different social classes talked with their children. Hart and Risley had both worked in preschool programs designed to boost the language skills of low-income kids, but they had been dissatisfied with the results of such efforts: the achievement gap between rich and poor had continued to widen. They decided to look beyond the classroom and examine what went on inside the home. Hart and Risley recruited forty-two families: thirteen upper, or \xe2\x80\x9cprofessional,\xe2\x80\x9d class, ten middle class, thirteen working class, and six on welfare. Each family had a baby who was between seven and twelve months old. During the next two and a half years, observers visited each home for an hour every month, and taped the encounters. They were like dinner guests who never said much but kept coming back.

In all, Hart and Risley reported, they analyzed \xe2\x80\x9cmore than 1,300 hours of casual interactions between parents and their language-learning children.\xe2\x80\x9d The researchers noticed many similarities among the families: \xe2\x80\x9cThey all disciplined their children and taught them good manners and how to dress and toilet themselves.\xe2\x80\x9d They all showed their children affection and said things like \xe2\x80\x9cDon\xe2\x80\x99t jump on the couch\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cUse your spoon\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9cDo you have to go potty?\xe2\x80\x9d But the researchers also found that the wealthier parents consistently talked more with their kids. Among the professional families, the average number of words that children heard in an hour was twenty-one hundred and fifty; among the working-class families, it was twelve hundred and fifty; among the welfare families, it was six hundred and twenty. Over time, these daily differences had major consequences, Hart and Risley concluded: \xe2\x80\x9cWith few exceptions, the more parents talked to their children, the faster the children\xe2\x80\x99s vocabularies were growing and the higher the children\xe2\x80\x99s I.Q. test scores at age 3 and later.\xe2\x80\x9d

Hart and Risley\xe2\x80\x99s research has grown in prominence, in part because large-scale educational reforms like No Child Left Behind have proved disappointing. Addressing the word gap by coaching new parents sounds like a simpler intervention. Last year, Hillary Clinton announced a new initiative, Too Small to Fail, that emphasizes the importance of talking to infants and young children; in the fall, President Barack Obama convened a White House conference whose goal was to \xe2\x80\x9cbridge the word gap and put more young people on the path to success.\xe2\x80\x9d Other cities, including Cambridge, Massachusetts, have initiated programs similar to the one in Providence, and still others have begun public-awareness campaigns with radio spots and bus-shelter signs reminding parents to talk frequently to their kids. The notion of the word gap even turned up on \xe2\x80\x9cOrange Is the New Black,\xe2\x80\x9d when one of the inmates urged her boyfriend to talk with their new daughter, because \xe2\x80\x9cthere\xe2\x80\x99s all these studies that say that if you don\xe2\x80\x99t talk to the baby they end up, like, fucked by the time they\xe2\x80\x99re five.\xe2\x80\x9d

The way you converse with your child is one of the most intimate aspects of parenting, shaped both by your personality and by cultural habits so deep that they can feel automatic. Changing how low-income parents interact with their children is a delicate matter, and not especially easy. Lissette Castrill\xc3\xb3n was sensitive to the challenge, and she had an appealing informality: she listened carefully to Rodriguez, praised her efforts, and said admiring things about Eilen, all while sitting cross-legged on the floor. But, perhaps inevitably, there was an awkward moment.

Castrill\xc3\xb3n had brought an iPad with her, and she played for Rodriguez a video of a mother shopping at the grocery store while her toddler sat in the cart\xe2\x80\x94just to show, Castrill\xc3\xb3n explained, that you could \xe2\x80\x9ctalk aloud when you\xe2\x80\x99re pretty much doing anything.\xe2\x80\x9d The mother onscreen was blond and fit, and wore white jeans; she looked like a character in a Nancy Meyers movie, and her patter was so constant that it became wearying. \xe2\x80\x9cHere\xe2\x80\x99s our crunchy peanut butter, sweetheart!\xe2\x80\x9d she trilled, scanning an aisle filled with organic food. \xe2\x80\x9cHere\xe2\x80\x99s the Wild Oats one. Roasted almond butter. Crunchy. Let\xe2\x80\x99s get crunchy, Bubba.\xe2\x80\x9d The cart was piled high, and the items looked expensive. \xe2\x80\x9cBubba, we\xe2\x80\x99re running out of room. What are we going to do? Did Mommy buy too many groceries today? I think we should get the creamy, too, because Murphy does not like when I get that crunchy. And we like to have the peanut butter because peanut butter\xe2\x80\x99s good for you. It\xe2\x80\x99s got protein.\xe2\x80\x9d

Rodriguez watched the video with a serious expression. It was hard to imagine her holding forth with such preening gusto in the organics aisle. Castrill\xc3\xb3n said, \xe2\x80\x9cWell, you know, just\xe2\x80\x94whatever the food is you\xe2\x80\x99re buying, you can talk about color, shape, and texture.\xe2\x80\x9d

In 2012, the mayor of Providence, Angel Taveras, heard about the Mayors Challenge, a new competition being offered to cities that proposed a bold idea for making urban life better. The prize was to be given by Bloomberg Philanthropies, the foundation started by Michael Bloomberg, the former New York mayor, on the premise that cities are \xe2\x80\x9cthe new laboratories of democracy.\xe2\x80\x9d The city that won the grand prize would receive five million dollars to realize its project, and four other cities would be given a million each. As Taveras recalled, \xe2\x80\x9cThey announced that challenge on Twitter, and right away I said, \xe2\x80\x98We\xe2\x80\x99re going to go for it.\xe2\x80\x99 And I didn\xe2\x80\x99t know exactly what it would be at the time, but I knew it was going to be on early-childhood education.\xe2\x80\x9d

Taveras\xe2\x80\x99s focus was not unusual: these days, everyone from preschool teachers to politicians talks about infant brain development, and toy companies tap into parental anxiety about it. But Taveras had a personal investment in the subject. He is the son of immigrants from the Dominican Republic, neither of whom went beyond the eighth grade. He grew up in Providence, and his mother, Amparo, who raised him largely on her own, worked factory jobs to support him and his two siblings. When he was four, Amparo enrolled him in a local Head Start program, and he felt that it had made a decisive difference in his life. He went on to Providence public schools, and then attended Harvard University and Georgetown law school. Taveras calls himself the \xe2\x80\x9cHead Start to Harvard\xe2\x80\x9d mayor, and he still has his graduation picture from the program. \xe2\x80\x9cI wore a cap and gown, and it was so special for me,\xe2\x80\x9d he recalled.

In 2010, at the age of forty, Taveras became the first Latino mayor of Providence, a city that is nineteen per cent Latino, mostly Dominican. Tall and skinny, with rimless eyeglasses, Taveras is nerdier and nicer than you might expect of a Providence mayor. One of his predecessors, Buddy Cianci, was twice convicted of felonies while in office: once for racketeering, and once for assaulting a man\xe2\x80\x94using a lit cigarette and a fireplace log\xe2\x80\x94who was dating his ex-wife. Taveras, by contrast, wrote a children\xe2\x80\x99s book called \xe2\x80\x9cHow to Do Well in School\xe2\x80\x9d and seems genuinely to enjoy mayoral duties like dropping in for \xe2\x80\x9cstory time\xe2\x80\x9d at a local library.

One day, while Taveras was mulling over what to propose for the Bloomberg competition, his policy director, Toby Shepherd, told him about Hart and Risley\xe2\x80\x99s research\xe2\x80\x94including their calculation that a poor four-year-old has heard thirty million fewer words from his parents than a wealthy one has.

That number had attracted a lot of attention in the press\xe2\x80\x94to the point that Hart and Risley\xe2\x80\x99s study was sometimes faulted for an overemphasis on the sheer quantity of words. But Taveras learned that Hart, who died in 2012, and Risley, who died in 2007, had also identified important differences in kinds of talk. In the recordings of the professional families, they found a \xe2\x80\x9cgreater richness of nouns, modifiers, and past-tense verbs,\xe2\x80\x9d and more conversations on subjects that children had initiated. Catherine Snow, a professor at Harvard\xe2\x80\x99s Graduate School of Education, who studies children\xe2\x80\x99s language development, told me that these findings made sense, since quantity was often a proxy for quality. \xe2\x80\x9cFamilies that talk a lot also talk about more different things,\xe2\x80\x9d Snow said. \xe2\x80\x9cThey use more grammatical variety in their sentences and more sophisticated vocabulary, and produce more utterances in connected chains.\xe2\x80\x9d Such parents, she noted, \xe2\x80\x9cdon\xe2\x80\x99t just say, \xe2\x80\x98That\xe2\x80\x99s a teapot.\xe2\x80\x99 They say, \xe2\x80\x98Oh, look, a teapot! Let\xe2\x80\x99s have a tea party! There\xe2\x80\x99s Raggedy Ann\xe2\x80\x94do you think she wants to come to our tea party? Does she like sugar in her tea?\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Parents who talk a lot with their young children ask them many questions, including ones to which they know the answer. (\xe2\x80\x9cIs that a ducky on your shirt?\xe2\x80\x9d) They reply to those devilish \xe2\x80\x9cWhy?\xe2\x80\x9d questions toddlers love with elaborate explanations. Erika Hoff, a developmental psychologist at Florida Atlantic University, has published studies about early language development whose results are similar to those of Hart and Risley. She recalled marvelling at \xe2\x80\x9cthe young professor mothers\xe2\x80\x9d at a university childcare center: \xe2\x80\x9cEverything was a topic of conversation. If they had to get out of the building in case of a fire, they\xe2\x80\x99d be so busy discussing the pros and cons with their toddlers that I kind of wondered if they\xe2\x80\x99d make it.\xe2\x80\x9d

Among the more affluent families studied by Hart and Risley, a higher proportion of the talk directed at children was affirming, which was defined to include not just compliments like \xe2\x80\x9cGood job!\xe2\x80\x9d but also responses in which parents repeat and build on a child\xe2\x80\x99s comments: \xe2\x80\x9cYes, it is a bunny! It\xe2\x80\x99s a bunny eating a carrot!\xe2\x80\x9d In those families, the average child heard thirty-two affirmations and five prohibitions (\xe2\x80\x9cStop that\xe2\x80\x9d; \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s the wrong way!\xe2\x80\x9d) per hour\xe2\x80\x94a ratio of six to one. For the kids in the working-class families, the ratio was twelve affirmatives to seven prohibitions, and in the welfare families it was five affirmatives to eleven prohibitions. Hart and Risley included one extended description of a mother from the poorest group, at home with her twenty-three-month-old daughter, Inge:

The mother returns; Inge sits on the couch beside her to watch TV and says something incomprehensible. Mother responds, \xe2\x80\x9cQuit copying off of me. You a copycat.\xe2\x80\x9d Inge says something incomprehensible, and her mother does not respond. Inge picks up her sister\xe2\x80\x99s purse from the couch. Her mother initiates, \xe2\x80\x9cYou better get out of her purse.\xe2\x80\x9d Inge continues to explore the purse and her mother initiates, \xe2\x80\x9cGet out of her purse.\xe2\x80\x9d Inge does not answer; she begins to take coins out of the purse and put them on the coffee table. Her mother initiates, \xe2\x80\x9cGive me that purse.\xe2\x80\x9d Inge continues to put coins on the table. Her mother initiates, \xe2\x80\x9cAnd the money.\xe2\x80\x9d Inge does not answer but gives her mother the purse.

Hart and Risley noted that the mother was \xe2\x80\x9cconcerned\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9caffectionate\xe2\x80\x9d toward her child. Inge was dressed in nice clothes and fed consistently, and she was toilet trained; at one point, the mother picked her up and kissed her. But she made \xe2\x80\x9cfew efforts to engage the child in conversation,\xe2\x80\x9d and did not \xe2\x80\x9cre-direct\xe2\x80\x9d Inge when she wanted her to stop doing something, or treat exploratory misbehavior as a sign of curiosity rather than defiance. Most of what the mother said to Inge was \xe2\x80\x9ccorrective or critical.\xe2\x80\x9d

Hart and Risley also provided examples of various kinds of conversation\xe2\x80\x94mostly, but not exclusively, among the professional families\xe2\x80\x94in which parents prompted and encouraged children to talk:

The mother initiates, asking Calvin (24 months), \xe2\x80\x9cWhat did we do on Halloween? What did you put on your head and on your body? What did you look like?\xe2\x80\x9d When Calvin does not answer, she tells him, \xe2\x80\x9cYou were a kitty cat.\xe2\x80\x9d Calvin says, \xe2\x80\x9cWanna get. Where go?\xe2\x80\x9d His mother says, \xe2\x80\x9cWhat are you looking for? I know what you\xe2\x80\x99re looking for. What used to be on the door handle?\xe2\x80\x9d Calvin says, \xe2\x80\x9cWhere?\xe2\x80\x9d His mother says, \xe2\x80\x9cThe trick-or-treat bag. We ate up all the candy already.\xe2\x80\x9d Calvin says, \xe2\x80\x9cWhere the candy go?\xe2\x80\x9d His mother says, \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s all gone in your tummy.\xe2\x80\x9d Calvin says, \xe2\x80\x9cWant some.\xe2\x80\x9d

Mayor Taveras thought that such conversational strategies could be taught to new parents, and decided to address the word-gap problem with the Mayors Challenge. \xe2\x80\x9cHead Start is awesome,\xe2\x80\x9d he told me. \xe2\x80\x9cBut we\xe2\x80\x99ve gotta do something even before Head Start.\xe2\x80\x9d At the time, his wife was pregnant with their first child, and he \xe2\x80\x9cwas reading and talking to my daughter in utero. I decided it can\xe2\x80\x99t hurt. I\xe2\x80\x99d come home and say, \xe2\x80\x98It\xe2\x80\x99s Daddy,\xe2\x80\x99 and \xe2\x80\x98How are you?,\xe2\x80\x99 and everything else.\xe2\x80\x9d

Even though the Hart and Risley study had encompassed just a few dozen families, the transcribing and coding of all those tapes had been laborious. New technology, Shepherd told him, could make counting words much easier. In 2005, a research foundation named LENA (for Language Environment Analysis) had developed a small digital device that could record for sixteen hours and recognize adult words, child vocalizations, and conversational turns. Such distinctions were important, because researchers had determined that merely overheard speech\xe2\x80\x94a mother holding a child on her lap but talking on the phone, for instance\xe2\x80\x94contributed less to language development. The LENA recorder could also distinguish between actual people speaking in a child\xe2\x80\x99s earshot and sounds from TVs and other electronic devices; children under the age of two appear to learn language only from other humans. The device was about the size of an iPod, and it fit into the pocket of a specially designed vest or pair of overalls. (Children soon forgot about the devices, though they occasionally ended up in the toilet or in the dog\xe2\x80\x99s bed.)

LENA\xe2\x80\x99s device had been used in academic research on language development and in interventions for hard-of-hearing, autistic, and developmentally delayed children. In 2009, a Chicago surgeon named Dana Suskind, who specializes in cochlear-implant surgery for deaf children, began using LENA\xe2\x80\x99s technology in a program called the Thirty Million Words Initiative, which includes a study on the effects of encouraging low-income parents to talk more with their children. Suskind had come across Hart and Risley\xe2\x80\x99s research after noticing divergent outcomes for her young patients. \xe2\x80\x9cCochlear implants are truly a modern medical miracle,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cBut, after the implantation surgery, some of the kids we saw were reading and speaking on grade level, and others were much slower to communicate. The difference almost always had to do with socioeconomic status.\xe2\x80\x9d

Taveras named his proposed project Providence Talks, and decided that technology would be supported with counselling. During home visits with low-income parents, caseworkers would discuss the science of early brain development. They\xe2\x80\x99d advise parents to try to understand better what their kids were feeling, instead of simply saying no. Parents would be told that, even when they were bathing a child or cooking dinner, they could be narrating what was going on, as well as singing, counting, and asking questions. The caseworkers would bring books and demonstrate how to read them: asking children questions about what was going to happen next and livening up the dialogue with funny, high-pitched voices and enthusiastic mooing and woofing.

For the mayor, it was important that Providence Talks did not seem exclusive. \xe2\x80\x9cI love it that you can do this in Spanish or any other language,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cI love it that you can do it even if you\xe2\x80\x99re not literate. Even if you can\xe2\x80\x99t read them a book. You can still talk to them about what an apple is: \xe2\x80\x98This is a red apple, this is a green apple, this is how you cut it.\xe2\x80\x99 Just talking and engaging and having a conversation.\xe2\x80\x9d

In March of 2013, Taveras learned that Providence Talks had won the Mayors Challenge grand prize. The Bloomberg committee praised the city for its \xe2\x80\x9cdirect, simple, and revolutionary approach.\xe2\x80\x9d Taveras wanted to jump up and down and scream, but, fearing that this wasn\xe2\x80\x99t mayoral, he contented himself with fist-bumping Toby Shepherd and the rest of his staff.

A big part of the program\xe2\x80\x99s appeal lay in its technology. Using LENA devices, caseworkers could show parents how much they\xe2\x80\x99d been talking at various times of the day. Crucially, parents found the gadgets fun: they were like Fitbits for conversation. Andrea Riquetti, the director of Providence Talks, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cThe fact that we have this report, in a graph form, makes it nonjudgmental.\xe2\x80\x9d Parents were likely to resist, she felt, if the program seemed scolding. \xe2\x80\x9cWe can say, look, here\xe2\x80\x99s the data. Look how much you were talking at eleven o\xe2\x80\x99clock! How can we do this for another half hour? As opposed to a home visitor telling a parent, \xe2\x80\x98You\xe2\x80\x99re not talking to your child enough.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

Providence Talks had its critics, some of whom thought that the program seemed too intrusive. The A.C.L.U. raised questions about what would happen to the recordings, and one of the organization\xe2\x80\x99s Rhode Island associates, Hillary Davis, told National Journal, \xe2\x80\x9cThere\xe2\x80\x99s always a concern when we walk in with technology into lower-income families, immigrant populations, minority populations, and we say, \xe2\x80\x98This will help you.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d She continued, \xe2\x80\x9cWe don\xe2\x80\x99t necessarily recognize the threat to their own safety or liberty that can accidentally come along with that.\xe2\x80\x9d

Others charged that Providence Talks was imposing middle-class cultural values on poorer parents who had their own valid approaches to raising children, and argued that the program risked faulting parents for their children\xe2\x80\x99s academic shortcomings while letting schools off the hook. Nobody contested the fact that, on average, low-income children entered kindergarten with fewer scholastic skills than kids who were better off, but there were many reasons for the disparity, ranging from poor nutrition to chaotic living conditions to the absence of a preschool education. In a caustic essay titled \xe2\x80\x9cSelling the Language Gap,\xe2\x80\x9d which was published in Anthropology News, Susan Blum, of Notre Dame, and Kathleen Riley, of Fordham, called Providence Talks an example of \xe2\x80\x9csilver-bullet thinking,\xe2\x80\x9d the latest in a long history of \xe2\x80\x9cblame-the-victim approaches to language and poverty.\xe2\x80\x9d

To some scholars, the program\xe2\x80\x99s emphasis on boosting numbers made it seem as though the quality of conversation didn\xe2\x80\x99t matter much. As James Morgan, a developmental psycholinguist at Brown University, put it, obsessive word counting might lead parents to conclude that \xe2\x80\x9csaying \xe2\x80\x98doggy, doggy, doggy, doggy\xe2\x80\x99 is more meaningful than saying \xe2\x80\x98doggy.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Kathy Hirsh-Pasek, a psychologist at Temple University, told me that Hart and Risley had \xe2\x80\x9cdone a very important piece of work that pointed to a central problem\xe2\x80\x9d; nevertheless, their findings had often been interpreted glibly, as if the solution were to let words \xe2\x80\x9cjust wash over a child, like the background noise of a TV.\xe2\x80\x9d Her own research, including a recent paper written with Lauren Adamson and other psychologists, points to the importance of interactions between parents and children in which they are both paying attention to the same thing\xe2\x80\x94a cement mixer on the street, a picture in a book\xe2\x80\x94and in which the ensuing conversation (some of which might be conducted in gestures) is fluid and happens over days, even weeks. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s not just serve and return,\xe2\x80\x9d Hirsh-Pasek said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s serve and return\xe2\x80\x94and return and return.\xe2\x80\x9d

The original Hart and Risley research, whose data set had only six families in the poorest category, was also called into question. Mark Liberman, a linguist at the University of Pennsylvania, said, \xe2\x80\x9cDo low-income people talk with their kids less? Well, that\xe2\x80\x99s a question about millions of people. Think of people in the survey business, trying to predict elections or develop a marketing campaign. They would find it laughable to draw conclusions without a large randomized sample.\xe2\x80\x9d Encouraging adults to talk more to children was all to the good, Liberman said, but it was important to remember that \xe2\x80\x9cthere are some wealthy people who don\xe2\x80\x99t talk to their children much and some poor people who talk a lot.\xe2\x80\x9d

Indeed, recent research that supports Hart and Risley\xe2\x80\x99s work has found a great deal of variability within classes. In 2006, researchers at the LENA Foundation recorded the conversations of three hundred and twenty-nine families, who were divided into groups by the mothers\xe2\x80\x99 education level, a reasonable proxy for social class. Like Hart and Risley, the LENA researchers determined that, on average, parents who had earned at least a B.A. spoke more around their children than other parents: 14,926 words per day versus 12,024. (They attributed Hart and Risley\xe2\x80\x99s bigger gap to the fact that they had recorded families only during the late afternoon and the evening\xe2\x80\x94when families talk most\xe2\x80\x94and extrapolated.) But the LENA team also found that some of the less educated parents spoke a lot more than some of the highly educated parents.

Anne Fernald, a psychologist at Stanford, has published several papers examining the influence of socioeconomic status on children\xe2\x80\x99s language development. In one recent study, Fernald, with a colleague, Adriana Weisleder, and others, identified \xe2\x80\x9clarge disparities\xe2\x80\x9d among socioeconomic groups in \xe2\x80\x9cinfants\xe2\x80\x99 language processing, speech production, and vocabulary.\xe2\x80\x9d But they also found big differences among working-class families, both in terms of \xe2\x80\x9cthe children\xe2\x80\x99s language proficiency and the parents\xe2\x80\x99 verbal engagement with the child.\xe2\x80\x9d Fernald, who sits on the scientific advisory board for Providence Talks, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cSome of the wealthiest families in our research had low word counts, possibly because they were on their gadgets all day. So you can see an intermingling at the extremes of rich and poor. Socioeconomic status is not destiny.\xe2\x80\x9d

In response to the privacy concerns, Mayor Taveras and his team volunteered their own households to be the first ones recorded. They also guaranteed that the LENA Foundation\xe2\x80\x99s software would erase the recordings after the algorithm analyzed the data. Though this probably reassured some families, it also disappointed some scholars. \xe2\x80\x9cThat\xe2\x80\x99s a huge amount of data being thrown out!\xe2\x80\x9d James Morgan, of Brown, told me. \xe2\x80\x9cThere were real concerns whether families would participate otherwise. But as a scientist it breaks my heart.\xe2\x80\x9d

To those who argued that Providence Talks embodied cultural imperialism, staff members responded that, on the contrary, they were \xe2\x80\x9cempowering\xe2\x80\x9d parents with knowledge. Andrea Riquetti, the Providence Talks director, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cIt really is our responsibility to let families know what it takes to succeed in the culture they live in. Which may not necessarily be the same as the culture they have. But it\xe2\x80\x99s their choice whether they decide to. It\xe2\x80\x99s not a case of our saying, \xe2\x80\x98You have to do this.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Riquetti grew up in Quito, Ecuador, came to America at the age of seventeen, and worked for many years as a kindergarten teacher in Providence schools. In Latino culture, she said, \xe2\x80\x9cthe school is seen as being in charge of teaching children their letters and all that, while parents are in charge of discipline\xe2\x80\x94making sure they listen and they\xe2\x80\x99re good and they sit still. Parents don\xe2\x80\x99t tend, overall, to give children a lot of choices and options. It\xe2\x80\x99s kind of like \xe2\x80\x98I rule the roost so that you can behave and learn at school.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d The Providence Talks approach \xe2\x80\x9cis a little more like \xe2\x80\x98No, your child and what they have to say is really important.\xe2\x80\x99 And having them feel really good about themselves as opposed to passive about their learning is important, because that\xe2\x80\x99s what\xe2\x80\x99s going to help them succeed in this culture.\xe2\x80\x9d

Riquetti and the Providence Talks team didn\xe2\x80\x99t seem troubled by the concerns that Hart and Risley\xe2\x80\x99s data set wasn\xe2\x80\x99t robust enough. Although no subsequent study has found a word gap as large as thirty million, several of them have found that children in low-income households have smaller vocabularies than kids in higher-income ones. This deficit correlates with the quantity and the quality of talk elicited by the adults at home, and becomes evident quite early\xe2\x80\x94in one study, when some kids were eighteen months old. Lack of conversation wasn\xe2\x80\x99t the only reason that low-income kids started out behind in school, but it was certainly a problem.

The biggest question was whether Providence Talks could really change something as personal, casual, and fundamental as how people talk to their babies. Erika Hoff, of Florida Atlantic University, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cIn some ways, parenting behavior clearly can change. I have a daughter who has a baby now and she does everything differently from how I did it\xe2\x80\x94putting babies to sleep on their backs, not giving them milk till they\xe2\x80\x99re a year old. But patterns of interacting are different. You\xe2\x80\x99re trying to get people to change something that seems natural to them and comes from a fairly deep place. I don\xe2\x80\x99t know how malleable that is.\xe2\x80\x9d

After decades of failed educational reforms, few policymakers are na\xc3\xafve enough to believe that a single social intervention could fully transform disadvantaged children\xe2\x80\x99s lives. The growing economic inequality in America is too entrenched, too structural. But that\xe2\x80\x99s hardly an argument for doing nothing. Although improvements in test scores associated with preschool programs fade as students proceed through elementary school, broader benefits can be seen many years later. A few oft-cited studies have shown that low-income kids who attended high-quality preschool programs were more likely to graduate from high school and less likely to become pregnant as teen-agers or to be incarcerated; they also earned more money, on average, than peers who were not in such programs. Such data suggest that a full assessment of Providence Talks will take decades to complete.

On a cool, rainy morning in April, I went on a home visit with a young caseworker named Stephanie Taveras (no relation to the mayor), who had been assigned to Providence Talks. Two months earlier, the program had begun with fifty-eight families; the plan was to start adding many more families in the fall, with a projected, if optimistic, enrollment of two thousand families. The monthly recording and coaching visits would go on for two years. On earlier visits, Taveras had discussed a baby\xe2\x80\x99s cognitive development by bringing a little wax model of a brain with her.

The family lived in an apartment in Southside, on a block of small, scrubby lawns, chain-link fences, and two-story wooden houses. It was a predominantly Latino neighborhood, where a third of the families have incomes below the poverty line. On a nearby street, there was a corner shop, Perla del Caribe, and a meat market, El Vecinos, but there was no one out on the street that morning, and it felt a little desolate.

Inside, Taveras greeted a seven-month-old girl, Skylah, who was smiling and gurgling while propped up in an ExerSaucer. Skylah\xe2\x80\x99s parents, Maranda Raposo and Nicholas Mailloux, were seated on a couch in a gray-carpeted living room whose walls were mostly bare. In one corner, a cat was curled up. In another, a TV was showing an episode of \xe2\x80\x9cLaw & Order: Special Victims Unit.\xe2\x80\x9d

Raposo, who was twenty-one, had long, magenta-tinted hair pulled back in a ponytail. She smiled sweetly but was soft-spoken and reticent with her guests. She had dropped out of high school when she was a freshman, after she got pregnant with her first child, Isabella, who was now five. Raposo was hoping to get her G.E.D., but in the meantime she was working two part-time jobs as a cashier, at Party City and at Sears. Mailloux, who was twenty-five and had a five-year-old son by a former girlfriend, was staying home with the kids. Raposo told me later that she had been willing to try Providence Talks because \xe2\x80\x9cit was something we could experience with Skylah\xe2\x80\x94it could bring us closer as a family.\xe2\x80\x9d Just as she and Mailloux wanted to help Skylah stand out by giving her a name with an unusual spelling, they wanted to feel that the time they spent with her was special. \xe2\x80\x9cSome people don\xe2\x80\x99t even bother talking to their kids,\xe2\x80\x9d Raposo said. \xe2\x80\x9cWe talk to her.\xe2\x80\x9d Nevertheless, before enrolling in the program she hadn\xe2\x80\x99t known \xe2\x80\x9cexactly how important that is.\xe2\x80\x9d

Taveras plopped down on the floor and, like Mary Poppins with her carpet bag, started pulling items out of her satchel. She handed Mailloux a board book, \xe2\x80\x9cPeek-a Who?,\xe2\x80\x9d and he put Skylah on his lap and started reading it to her: \xe2\x80\x9cWho do you think it\xe2\x80\x99s gonna be?\xe2\x80\x9d Skylah patted the book and giggled.

Taveras showed them graphs generated by the previous month\xe2\x80\x99s recording, noting that their words and conversational turns had gone down a bit. \xe2\x80\x9cI went to the nail salon that day,\xe2\x80\x9d Raposo recalled. \xe2\x80\x9cEverybody was talking to her, but she was just, like, staring.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cShe wasn\xe2\x80\x99t in a talking mood that day,\xe2\x80\x9d Mailloux added.

\xe2\x80\x9cThat happens,\xe2\x80\x9d Taveras reassured them. \xe2\x80\x9cWhat\xe2\x80\x99s important is that you challenge yourself to do better the next time.\xe2\x80\x9d At one point, she asked, \xe2\x80\x9cAre there particular times of the day when you read to her? How many times a day, would you say?\xe2\x80\x9d

Both parents seemed a little vague on this point. After a moment, Raposo said, \xe2\x80\x9cWe try to do it more than once.\xe2\x80\x9d

Taveras asked them what they thought Skylah was learning when they read to her.

\xe2\x80\x9cColors, shapes, animals,\xe2\x80\x9d Raposo said.

\xe2\x80\x9cYes, and also she\xe2\x80\x99s learning about relationships,\xe2\x80\x9d Taveras said. \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re teaching her that she\xe2\x80\x99s important to you. You\xe2\x80\x99re making her feel good about herself.\xe2\x80\x9d Both parents nodded. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd educational skills, too. When she gets to school, she\xe2\x80\x99s gonna already be used to sitting still and paying attention.\xe2\x80\x9d

Taveras told them, \xe2\x80\x9cBabies at this age like books with large photos, bright pictures, simple drawings, and familiar things.\xe2\x80\x9d She recommended board books, noting, \xe2\x80\x9cPaper books she\xe2\x80\x99s gonna want to tear and chew.\xe2\x80\x9d

Taveras then offered some thoughts on how to read a book to a baby: \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s a four-part interaction. Get your book, point to something in the book\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x98Look, Skylah, a ball!\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x94ask a labelling question\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x98What\xe2\x80\x99s that? That\xe2\x80\x99s a cow! Moo! Can you say moo?\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x94and acknowledge her response. Like, if she babbles or makes a noise, make it back to her, so she knows you heard her. And, if you correct, do it positively. If you say, \xe2\x80\x98What\xe2\x80\x99s that?,\xe2\x80\x99 and she says, \xe2\x80\x98A dog,\xe2\x80\x99 you could say, \xe2\x80\x98It looks like a dog,\xe2\x80\x99 or \xe2\x80\x98It\xe2\x80\x99s brown like the dog, but it\xe2\x80\x99s a monkey!\xe2\x80\x99 That makes her feel good. Not just \xe2\x80\x98No,\xe2\x80\x99 or \xe2\x80\x98No, that\xe2\x80\x99s a monkey.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d

Raposo nodded again, but she seemed most comfortable quietly watching Taveras, who remained on the floor, singing and clapping with Skylah.

Though cultural factors may well explain why some low-income parents talk relatively little with their toddlers, the most obvious explanation is poverty itself. When daily life is stressful and uncertain and dispiriting, it can be difficult to summon up the patience and the playfulness for an open-ended conversation with a small, persistent, possibly whiny child. In 2007, Richard Weissbourd, a senior lecturer at the Harvard Graduate School of Education, helped establish a campaign in Boston that urged parents to talk to their kids, and he organized focus groups with low-income parents. \xe2\x80\x9cYou had some people working three jobs or dealing with the steady drizzle of helplessness and hopelessness,\xe2\x80\x9d he recalled. \xe2\x80\x9cThat makes it hard to have vibrant conversations with a baby. They\xe2\x80\x99d say, \xe2\x80\x98Look, when I get home I have to clean and cook and do the laundry.\xe2\x80\x99 They\xe2\x80\x99re exhausted. They\xe2\x80\x99d say, \xe2\x80\x98Sometimes we have to put our kids in front of the TV.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Weissbourd said of interventions like Providence Talks, \xe2\x80\x9cMaybe we have the model wrong. Maybe what we need to do is come in and bring dinner and help with laundry and free up a parent to engage in more play with their child.\xe2\x80\x9d

Patricia Kuhl, a co-director of the Institute for Learning and Brain Science at the University of Washington, has studied \xe2\x80\x9cmotherese,\xe2\x80\x9d the brightly inflected talk that mothers, whatever their native language, direct at their babies, and that babies love. (Fathers and other adults, of course, are equally capable of saying \xe2\x80\x9cSooooo big!\xe2\x80\x9d in a singsong voice.) Kuhl told me, \xe2\x80\x9cMotherese, when you combine that with being one on one with a baby, is dynamite for language development.\xe2\x80\x9d Parents are paying full attention, speaking in that high, lilting voice for maximum reaction, giving babies a chance to babble and coo back. But, Kuhl added, \xe2\x80\x9cMotherese is, by nature, happy talk. If you\xe2\x80\x99re stressed or depressed, it can be hard to get into that mode.\xe2\x80\x9d

Then, too, some parents may not see the point of talking to babies, who can\xe2\x80\x99t yet speak, or even of talking much to toddlers, who do, but sometimes unintelligibly. Andrea Riquetti told me, \xe2\x80\x9cI think educated people are more aware of the importance of communication and interaction and language.\xe2\x80\x9d In some families, she said, \xe2\x80\x9cif a baby\xe2\x80\x99s really \xe2\x80\x98good\xe2\x80\x99 they get to spend a lot of time alone in their crib.\xe2\x80\x9d

When I asked myself why I had talked a lot with my babies\xe2\x80\x94and had read aloud favorite picture books to the point that I could recite them from memory\xe2\x80\x94I realized that I hadn\xe2\x80\x99t been driven mainly by knowledge of brain development or by pedagogical intent. It was just that talking made the daily labor of mothering more interesting. Long stretches of time with toddlers can be boring, and the unavoidable moments when you admonished and corrected them were, to me, the dullest. It was more fun if you satisfied your own intellectual curiosity along with theirs: reading books about African animals or Chinese New Year celebrations; trying to remember why the sky is blue; honing age-appropriate arguments for eating your carrots.

When a family places a very high value on discipline and respect for parental authority, there is often disapproval of talking back, which can inhibit conversation in general. To some extent, this attitude tracks with class, perhaps because many working-class parents, consciously or not, are preparing children for jobs and lives in which they will not have a lot of power or autonomy. The sociologist Annette Lareau, in her classic 2003 study, \xe2\x80\x9cUnequal Childhoods,\xe2\x80\x9d interviewed the parents of eighty-eight nine- and ten-year-old children, then closely followed twelve of these families in order to compare the child-rearing styles of middle-class parents with those of poor and working-class parents. The middle-class families she observed practiced what she called \xe2\x80\x9cconcerted cultivation\xe2\x80\x9d: enrolling kids in various organized activities led by adults, but also engaging even young kids in a lot of back-and-forth conversation with adults. Working-class and poor families favored an \xe2\x80\x9caccomplishment of natural growth\xe2\x80\x9d approach. Their children\xe2\x80\x99s lives were less customized to their preferences or to their parents\xe2\x80\x99 notions of how to develop their particular talents; discipline came in the form of directives and, sometimes, threats of physical punishment; talk was less extensive and less geared toward drawing out a child\xe2\x80\x99s opinions.

When I asked Lareau, who teaches at the University of Pennsylvania, about the language aspect of her research, she said, \xe2\x80\x9cThe class differences in the amount of speech inside the families really surprised me.\xe2\x80\x9d She recalled that a white working-class girl in her study once brought up a weighty spiritual matter with her parents: \xe2\x80\x9cWe were sitting in their completely comfortable, pleasant living room. The girl was all excited. She said, \xe2\x80\x98Do you know what a mortal sin is?\xe2\x80\x99 The parents said, \xe2\x80\x98You tell us.\xe2\x80\x99 They listened to her answer, said nothing in reply, and went back to watching TV.\xe2\x80\x9d

In middle-class families, Lareau frequently witnessed the kind of verbal jousting between parents and children that gives kids a certain intellectual confidence. One upper-middle-class African-American family she spent time with\xe2\x80\x94Terry, a trial lawyer; his wife, Christina, a corporate executive; and their nine-year-old son, Alexander\xe2\x80\x94was especially fond of these kinds of debate. In one conversation, Terry playfully challenged his son to defend his list of favorite cars: \xe2\x80\x9cLast time, you said the Miata, the Mercedes, and the Bugatti. Which one is it?\xe2\x80\x9d Alex replied, \xe2\x80\x9cThis is America. It\xe2\x80\x99s my prerogative to change my mind if I want to.\xe2\x80\x9d

Lareau did not see the middle-class approach as inherently superior. \xe2\x80\x9cThe amount of talk in those households is exhausting,\xe2\x80\x9d she said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt involves a lot of labor on the parents\xe2\x80\x99 part, and sometimes parents are really not enjoying it. Sometimes kids use their verbal acuity to be really mean to each other.\xe2\x80\x9d She often found the kids in poor and working-class families to be more polite to their elders, less whiny, more competent, and more independent than their middle-class counterparts. Still, Lareau concluded, the kind of talk that prevailed in middle-class households offered better preparation for success in school and in professional careers. It taught children to debate, extemporize, and advocate for themselves, and it helped them develop the vocabulary that tends to reap academic rewards.

James Morgan, the Brown University linguist, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cIf you\xe2\x80\x99re mainly confined to \xe2\x80\x98Eat your food,\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x98Chew every bite,\xe2\x80\x99 there are going to be fewer words heard at the dinner table. As opposed to starting a conversation with \xe2\x80\x98Hey, did you hear the blue whales are making a comeback off California?,\xe2\x80\x99 or \xe2\x80\x98Oh, they just discovered a huge new dinosaur.\xe2\x80\x99 And, after all, almost all little kids are interested in subjects like that.\xe2\x80\x9d

Asking such questions often depends on having an education. But it\xe2\x80\x99s not just the topics\xe2\x80\x94it\xe2\x80\x99s the mode of inquiry. Anne Fernald said, \xe2\x80\x9cAs an educated mother, you have more experience with teacher talk, which is necessarily more abstract, because kids don\xe2\x80\x99t share common ground when they come to school. Education helps you learn how to make yourself clear to people who are outside your point of view.\xe2\x80\x9d

Last summer, I returned to Providence to see how the campaign was working for the families I\xe2\x80\x99d met in April. Andrea Riquetti, the program director, and Stephanie Taveras, the caseworker, took me back to the home of Maranda Raposo and Nicholas Mailloux. Skylah was now ten months old, and even more adorable, but the latest data were disappointing: the number of conversational turns and the over-all word count weren\xe2\x80\x99t as high as Taveras would have liked to see.

Mailloux told her, \xe2\x80\x9cAs soon as that vest goes on, she quiets down.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cAre you onto us, Skylah?\xe2\x80\x9d Taveras asked, smiling.

Mailloux pointed to an uptick on the chart. \xe2\x80\x9cI sang to her at ten o\xe2\x80\x99clock.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cLook at that!\xe2\x80\x9d Taveras said.

Raposo\xe2\x80\x99s older daughter, Isabella, was sitting on Mailloux\xe2\x80\x99s lap, watching the Disney Channel. Skylah crawled over to her and bopped her gleefully with the remote control.

\xe2\x80\x9cStop being so mean!\xe2\x80\x9d Raposo told her.

Riquetti stepped in to offer a benign interpretation of Skylah\xe2\x80\x99s behavior: \xe2\x80\x9cShe\xe2\x80\x99s saying, \xe2\x80\x98Pay attention to me.\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d Soon afterward, Skylah, grinning, dropped the remote control, and the batteries rolled under the couch. \xe2\x80\x9cThis is the age where they\xe2\x80\x99re trying to see how gravity works,\xe2\x80\x9d Riquetti explained. The remote was put back together and the TV was turned off. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s cause and effect. She\xe2\x80\x99s trying to make you\xe2\x80\x94\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cWork,\xe2\x80\x9d Raposo said.

Riquetti laughed sympathetically, then asked her how much time they spent reading with Skylah. Raposo answered firmly: thirty minutes a day.

The TV was back on again. Riquetti had told me that asking families to leave the set off could seem intrusive and high-handed. The staff at Providence Talks had hoped that, once parents saw data showing how much less conversation took place when the TV was on, they would leave it off more often. But the habit wasn\xe2\x80\x99t so easy to break.

On this visit, both parents seemed more attuned to Skylah\xe2\x80\x99s efforts to express herself, and more confident in their efforts to guide her. It was hard to say if this was because Skylah was older and more vocal or because Providence Talks had taught them to interact with her in richer ways. When I asked them about the change, Mailloux gave the program credit. \xe2\x80\x9cIt helps us learn more of how she understands things and reacts to them,\xe2\x80\x9d he said. \xe2\x80\x9cAnd . . .\xe2\x80\x9d He paused, flustered. \xe2\x80\x9cI don\xe2\x80\x99t know how to put it into words. It\xe2\x80\x99s in my head, but it won\xe2\x80\x99t come out.\xe2\x80\x9d

As part of the visit, Taveras was going through a developmental checklist for Skylah. One of the questions was \xe2\x80\x9cDoes she express pleasure and displeasure?\xe2\x80\x9d

Both parents nodded vigorously. \xe2\x80\x9cIf there\xe2\x80\x99s something she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t want, she screams and throws it,\xe2\x80\x9d Raposo said. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s so funny.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cDoes she play with sounds, like vocal play?\xe2\x80\x9d Taveras asked. They nodded.

\xe2\x80\x9cWhat do you do when she does that?\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cCopy it,\xe2\x80\x9d Mailloux said.

\xe2\x80\x9cPerfect.\xe2\x80\x9d

Raposo picked up a board book that contained pictures of animals. \xe2\x80\x9cWhere\xe2\x80\x99s the mouse?\xe2\x80\x9d She took Skylah\xe2\x80\x99s finger and gently placed it on the correct image. \xe2\x80\x9cRight there!\xe2\x80\x9d she said, in motherese.

Mailloux pointed to a picture of a messy room in the book. \xe2\x80\x9cThat looks like your brother and sister\xe2\x80\x99s room, doesn\xe2\x80\x99t it?\xe2\x80\x9d he joked.

Riquetti said, \xe2\x80\x9cYou\xe2\x80\x99re pointing and labelling and talking to her constantly. It\xe2\x80\x99s great. It\xe2\x80\x99s so important that you do what you do. You\xe2\x80\x99re making her smart when you talk to her.\xe2\x80\x9d

Mailloux looked a little sheepish. \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s out of respect,\xe2\x80\x9d he told Riquetti. \xe2\x80\x9cYou guys do your part, and we gotta do ours.\xe2\x80\x9d

In Angel Taveras\xe2\x80\x99s proposal to Bloomberg Philanthropies, he promised that Providence Talks would have a research component. Its results would be monitored and studied by a Brown University professor of educational policy, Kenneth Wong. The results of that study won\xe2\x80\x99t be published for some time, since the interventions are supposed to last for two years, and Providence Talks is only now expanding from its original pilot study of fifty-eight families. But there had been some analysis of the data from the first families, and Rob Horowitz, a spokesman for Providence Talks, told me, \xe2\x80\x9cWe are seeing early but promising preliminary results. More specifically, families that started with low word counts are showing increases of about fifty per cent in daily word counts and thirty per cent in conversational turns. The improvement is not as marked for families that began the program with above-average word counts.\xe2\x80\x9d

Of course, the hard numbers are only part of what you\xe2\x80\x99d want to know: to assess how successful an intervention like Providence Talks had been, you\xe2\x80\x99d have to look at whether the kids in the program entered kindergarten readier to learn, with bigger vocabularies than those of children in a control group. Wong and his team are looking at these questions.

The caseworkers at Providence Talks had impressed me with their sunny, gentle directives, but I wasn\xe2\x80\x99t sure if they could effect sweeping changes in the children\xe2\x80\x99s lives. Many of the core aspects of a parent\xe2\x80\x99s conversational style would be hard to alter, from grammar to vocabulary. And it didn\xe2\x80\x99t seem easy to revise, say, a parent\xe2\x80\x99s relationship to books. Riquetti had told me about a mother in the program who came to her crying because she had never read a book to her toddler. Since the child couldn\xe2\x80\x99t read, she hadn\xe2\x80\x99t seen the point of turning the pages together, looking at the pictures. Now she would try it, but she wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t be drawing on what the linguistic anthropologist Shirley Brice Heath had described to me as the \xe2\x80\x9cromantic memory, the nostalgia, of books being read to you when you were a child.\xe2\x80\x9d Even if you succeeded in getting such parents to read books regularly, the effect of the intervention would be minimal compared with, say, helping somebody like Maranda Raposo go back and finish her education. The last time I spoke with Raposo, she told me that, with two jobs and her kids to care for, she didn\xe2\x80\x99t have time to study for her G.E.D. When I tried, on several occasions, to contact the family again, I couldn\xe2\x80\x99t reach them\xe2\x80\x94their phones didn\xe2\x80\x99t seem to be working\xe2\x80\x94and Stephanie Taveras thought that they might have moved.

Providence Talks had more obvious value if you saw it as the beginning of a series of sustained interventions. Some of the children will likely attend preschool programs that will help them build on any language gains. Providence Talks will also help identify kids who could benefit from speech therapy and other support. Mayor Taveras told me he hopes that this integrated approach will become a model for the rest of the country.

The word \xe2\x80\x9cempowering\xe2\x80\x9d is overused, but a clear strength of Providence Talks is that it seemed to instill confidence in parents. Those rising graphs promised that parents could make a demonstrable difference in their children\xe2\x80\x99s lives. The parents I met did not seem to feel chided by the data, and they liked the idea of competing with their partners or themselves to log higher word counts.

One night in Providence, I had dinner with Andrea Riquetti and Toby Shepherd, the official who had first told Mayor Taveras about the word-gap problem. \xe2\x80\x9cProvidence Talks is not a panacea,\xe2\x80\x9d Shepherd said. \xe2\x80\x9cThese families face all kinds of challenges\xe2\x80\x94unemployment or whatever it is. My hope is that it\xe2\x80\x99s a helpful tool.\xe2\x80\x9d

Riquetti said, \xe2\x80\x9cIt\xe2\x80\x99s a chance to talk with parents about how they can positively interact with their kids. Sometimes in their busy lives, their stressful lives, they miss out on that.\xe2\x80\x9d The goal, she said, was to help parents \xe2\x80\x9cfeel they can make a difference when everything else kind of sucks.\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x99\xa6
